name: Justice Defenders
mission: Defending justice with defenceless communities in Africa through legal education, training, and practice.
theoryOfChange: Justice Defenders has a published 'Theory of Change' as part of their 'The Justice Defenders Way' document on their website. This document outlines their approach, values, and 2025-2027 strategy. Their approach involves working inside prisons to provide legal education, training, and practice, aiming to transform prisons into places of learning and service and build a movement that reimagines justice from the inside out. They explicitly state, 'Our 2025-2027 strategy will grow our movement of defending the defenceless. Over the next three years, we will shift power within more prisons and continue to reshape justice systems from the inside out. We will expand legal education and practice, create new pathways from incarceration to advocacy, and advance relational systems change that restores trust across justice institutions.' An external case study also confirms that Justice Defenders developed a 'powerful theory of change' through workshops and facilitated sessions.

judge_issues.message: The rationale mentions a high data confidence and Charity Navigator score, but it doesn't adequately address the significant deductions due to financial health (low reserves, unsustainable spending) and governance (CEO compensation concerns). These factors heavily influence the impact score and should be more explicitly discussed.

zakat.claim_evidence: There is no explicit mention of "zakat" on the provided information about Justice Defenders (USA) or its donation methods. The organization is described as a US nonprofit working in Africa, and while it accepts donations, there is no indication of a dedicated zakat fund or campaign by the US entity. (Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Justice_Defenders)

all_citations.claim: Justice Defenders holds a Charity Navigator score of 91.0/100.
The organization reported total revenue of $1,331,729 and a program expense ratio of 58.5%.
The organization facilitates law degree graduates and releases through alternative dispute resolution.
Justice Defenders provides legal education and training to help clients achieve release.
The organization trains paralegals and supports individuals in securing their release from prison.
The organization accepts general charitable donations (sadaqah).

dimension_explanations.alignment: Justice Defenders addresses the urgent cause of legal advocacy in underserved regions, though it has a low donor fit for those seeking traditional Muslim-focused giving pathways [1]. Contributions to the organization are considered sadaqah-eligible for those wishing to support general justice initiatives [8].
dimension_explanations.impact: The organization focuses on capacity building by training paralegals and facilitating law degrees for incarcerated individuals, resulting in numerous releases through legal support and ADR [3][5]. While it maintains a high data confidence and a Charity Navigator score of 91.0/100, its program expense ratio is 58.5% [1][2].

baseline_narrative.headline: Justice Defenders empowers incarcerated individuals and prison staff in Africa through legal education and training to ensure fair access to justice.
strengths: High financial accountability with a Charity Navigator score of 91.0/100

Efficient fundraising operations spending only $0.17 per $1 raised
baseline_narrative.summary: Justice Defenders (USA) is a non-profit organization dedicated to defending justice within defenseless communities in Africa by providing legal education, training, and practice [3][4]. The organization operates with a mission to equip prisoners and prison officers with the skills to provide legal services to those who cannot afford representation [5].

impact_evidence.evidence_grade_explanation: Strong outcome tracking with specific data on releases and graduates, though no independent RCTs are cited.
impact_evidence.theory_of_change_summary: By training prisoners and staff as legal professionals, the charity creates an internal legal service within the prison system to secure fair hearings.
impact_evidence.why_evidence_matters: Tracking releases ensures that legal training actually translates into freedom for the defenseless.

strengths.detail: The organization holds a <cite id="9">Platinum Seal of Transparency from Candid</cite> and a <cite id="2">91/100 rating from Charity Navigator</cite>.
Justice Defenders maintains <cite id="10">9.2 months of working capital</cite>, providing a significant buffer for operational continuity.
Their programs have successfully trained <cite id="11">370 paralegals</cite> and seen <cite id="12">47 law degree graduates</cite> within the prison system.

strengths_deep_dive: Justice Defenders excels in organizational transparency, evidenced by their <cite id="9">Candid Platinum Seal</cite> and high accountability scores. Their financial management is characterized by <cite id="13">efficient fundraising operations</cite>, spending only <cite id="14">$0.12 to raise every $1</cite>. This efficiency allows more resources to be directed toward their unique legal education model. Furthermore, their impact is highly measurable; they have facilitated the release of <cite id="15">over 11,000 individuals</cite> in a single reporting year through direct legal support and paralegal intervention.
rich_narrative.summary: Justice Defenders is a specialized legal advocacy organization that empowers incarcerated individuals and prison staff in Africa through legal education and professional training. Founded in <cite id="1">2007</cite>, the charity addresses the systemic lack of legal representation by transforming prisoners and guards into paralegals and lawyers. They maintain a <cite id="2">91/100 overall score from Charity Navigator</cite>, indicating a high level of accountability and transparency. The organization operates primarily in <cite id="3">Kenya and Uganda</cite>, focusing on communities where access to justice is often a luxury. By providing <cite id="4">University of London law degrees</cite> to those behind bars, they create a sustainable model of legal self-sufficiency. Their work has led to significant outcomes, including <cite id="5">15,338 prisoners released</cite> and over <cite id="6">2,032 individuals released through Alternative Dispute Resolution (ADR)</cite> as reported in their latest annual data. While their <cite id="7">program expense ratio of 58.5%</cite> is lower than some humanitarian peers, this reflects a model focused on intensive capacity building and long-term educational investment rather than simple commodity distribution. The charity reported <cite id="8">$1,868,837 in total revenue</cite> for the 2023 fiscal year, demonstrating a stable but niche operation in the international justice sector.
